---
title: "String Operations"
subtitle: "Lesson 8"
---

::: {.learning-objectives}
#### Learning Objectives

After this lesson, you will be able to:

- Use the `.str` accessor for vectorized string operations
- Apply common string methods (upper, lower, strip, replace)
- Use regex with `str.extract()` and `str.replace()`
- Handle case normalization challenges (UFAS vs Workday)
- Clean messy text data efficiently
:::

## Introduction

The UW salary data presents a real-world string cleaning challenge: the transition from UFAS to Workday changed name formatting from UPPERCASE to Title Case, and division names use different abbreviations. This lesson covers the tools you need to handle such inconsistencies.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Sample data with string cleaning challenges
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "last_name_ufas": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "O'BRIEN", "WILLIAMS-JONES", "MC DONALD"],
    "last_name_workday": ["Smith", "Johnson", "O'Brien", "Williams-Jones", "McDonald"],
    "division_ufas": ["Coll of Ltrs & Sci", "Sch of Med & Public Health", "Coll of Engr"],
    "division_workday": ["College of Letters & Science", "School of Medicine and Public Health", "College of Engineering"],
    "jobcode": ["FA001U", "AS002A", "FA003X", "CL001N", "ET002"]
})
df
```

## The `.str` Accessor

pandas provides vectorized string methods through the `.str` accessor:

```{python}
# Convert to uppercase
df["upper_workday"] = df["last_name_workday"].str.upper()

# Convert to lowercase
df["lower_ufas"] = df["last_name_ufas"].str.lower()

# Title case
df["title_ufas"] = df["last_name_ufas"].str.title()

df[["last_name_ufas", "last_name_workday", "upper_workday", "title_ufas"]]
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# stringr approach
library(stringr)
str_to_upper(df$name)
str_to_lower(df$name)
str_to_title(df$name)

# Base R
toupper(df$name)
tolower(df$name)
```
:::

## Common String Methods

### Stripping Whitespace

```{python}
messy = pd.Series(["  SMITH  ", "JOHNSON ", " WILLIAMS"])

print("Original:", messy.tolist())
print("Stripped:", messy.str.strip().tolist())
print("Left strip:", messy.str.lstrip().tolist())
print("Right strip:", messy.str.rstrip().tolist())
```

### String Length

```{python}
df["name_length"] = df["last_name_ufas"].str.len()
df[["last_name_ufas", "name_length"]]
```

### Checking String Content

```{python}
# Contains a pattern
df["has_hyphen"] = df["last_name_ufas"].str.contains("-")
print("Contains hyphen:")
print(df[["last_name_ufas", "has_hyphen"]])
```

```{python}
# Starts with / ends with
df["starts_mc"] = df["last_name_ufas"].str.startswith("MC")
df["ends_s"] = df["last_name_ufas"].str.endswith("S")
print("Starts with MC or ends with S:")
print(df[["last_name_ufas", "starts_mc", "ends_s"]])
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# stringr
str_detect(df$name, "-")
str_starts(df$name, "MC")
str_ends(df$name, "S")
str_length(df$name)
```
:::

## Replacing Text

### Simple Replacement

```{python}
# Replace specific text
divisions = df["division_ufas"].copy()
divisions = divisions.str.replace("Coll", "College")
divisions = divisions.str.replace("Ltrs", "Letters")
divisions = divisions.str.replace("Sci", "Science")
divisions = divisions.str.replace("Engr", "Engineering")
print(divisions)
```

### Chained Replacements

```{python}
# Method chaining for multiple replacements
cleaned = (
    df["division_ufas"]
    .str.replace("Coll", "College")
    .str.replace("Ltrs", "Letters")
    .str.replace("Sci", "Science")
    .str.replace("Engr", "Engineering")
    .str.replace("Sch", "School")
    .str.replace("Med", "Medicine")
)
print(cleaned)
```

## Regular Expressions

For more complex patterns, use regex:

```{python}
# Extract year from a string
dates = pd.Series(["Hired 2020-08-15", "Started 2018-03-01", "Began 2015-11-20"])
years = dates.str.extract(r"(\d{4})")
print(years)
```

```{python}
# Extract multiple groups
pattern = r"(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})"
date_parts = dates.str.extract(pattern)
date_parts.columns = ["year", "month", "day"]
date_parts
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# stringr with regex
str_extract(dates, "\\d{4}")
str_match(dates, "(\\d{4})-(\\d{2})-(\\d{2})")
```
:::

### Common Regex Patterns

| Pattern | Matches | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `\d` | Any digit | `\d{4}` matches "2024" |
| `\w` | Word character | `\w+` matches "Smith" |
| `\s` | Whitespace | `\s+` matches spaces/tabs |
| `.` | Any character | `.*` matches anything |
| `[A-Z]` | Capital letters | `[A-Z]+` matches "SMITH" |
| `^` | Start of string | `^Dr` matches "Dr Smith" |
| `$` | End of string | `son$` matches "Johnson" |

### Regex Replacement

```{python}
# Remove letter suffixes from jobcodes (U, A, X, N)
jobcodes = df["jobcode"].copy()
print("Before:", jobcodes.tolist())

# Remove single letter at end
cleaned_codes = jobcodes.str.replace(r"[UAXN]$", "", regex=True)
print("After:", cleaned_codes.tolist())
```

## Case Study: Name Normalization

The UFAS to Workday transition changed name formatting. Let's handle this:

```{python}
# Create normalization function
def normalize_name(name):
    """Normalize name to lowercase for matching."""
    if pd.isna(name):
        return None
    # Remove spaces in patterns like "MC DONALD" -> "MCDONALD"
    name = name.replace(" ", "")
    # Lowercase
    return name.lower()

# Apply to both formats
df["name_normalized_ufas"] = df["last_name_ufas"].apply(normalize_name)
df["name_normalized_workday"] = df["last_name_workday"].apply(normalize_name)

# Check if they match
df["names_match"] = df["name_normalized_ufas"] == df["name_normalized_workday"]

df[["last_name_ufas", "last_name_workday", "name_normalized_ufas", "names_match"]]
```

## Case Study: Division Name Normalization

Division names use different conventions:

```{python}
# Create a mapping for division abbreviations
division_mapping = {
    "Coll": "College",
    "Ltrs": "Letters",
    "Sci": "Science",
    "Sch": "School",
    "Med": "Medicine",
    "Engr": "Engineering",
    "&": "and",
}

def expand_abbreviations(text, mapping):
    """Expand abbreviations in text."""
    for abbrev, full in mapping.items():
        text = text.replace(abbrev, full)
    return text

# Apply
df["division_expanded"] = df["division_ufas"].apply(
    lambda x: expand_abbreviations(x, division_mapping)
)

df[["division_ufas", "division_workday", "division_expanded"]]
```

### Building a Robust Normalizer

```{python}
import re

def normalize_division(name):
    """
    Normalize division name for consistent comparison.

    Steps:
    1. Lowercase
    2. Expand common abbreviations
    3. Remove extra whitespace
    4. Remove punctuation
    """
    if pd.isna(name):
        return None

    # Lowercase
    name = name.lower()

    # Expand abbreviations
    expansions = [
        (r"\bcoll\b", "college"),
        (r"\bltrs\b", "letters"),
        (r"\bsci\b", "science"),
        (r"\bsch\b", "school"),
        (r"\bmed\b", "medicine"),
        (r"\bpub\b", "public"),
        (r"\bhlth\b", "health"),
        (r"\bengr\b", "engineering"),
        (r"\badmin\b", "administration"),
    ]

    for pattern, replacement in expansions:
        name = re.sub(pattern, replacement, name)

    # Normalize "and" / "&"
    name = name.replace("&", "and")

    # Remove extra whitespace
    name = " ".join(name.split())

    return name

# Test
test_names = [
    "Coll of Ltrs & Sci",
    "College of Letters and Science",
    "College of Letters & Science"
]

for n in test_names:
    print(f"{n:40} -> {normalize_division(n)}")
```

## Splitting and Joining

### Splitting Strings

```{python}
# Split on delimiter
full_names = pd.Series(["SMITH, JOHN", "JOHNSON, MARY", "WILLIAMS, JAMES"])
split = full_names.str.split(", ", expand=True)
split.columns = ["last", "first"]
split
```

```{python}
# Access specific part without expanding
full_names.str.split(", ").str[0]  # Last names only
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# tidyr
separate(df, full_name, into = c("last", "first"), sep = ", ")

# stringr
str_split(names, ", ", simplify = TRUE)
```
:::

### Joining Strings

```{python}
# Concatenate columns
df["full_name"] = df["last_name_workday"] + ", " + df["last_name_workday"].str[0] + "."
df["full_name"]
```

```{python}
# Join list elements
parts = pd.Series([["College", "of", "Engineering"], ["School", "of", "Business"]])
parts.str.join(" ")
```

## Handling Missing Values

String methods handle NaN gracefully:

```{python}
mixed = pd.Series(["SMITH", None, "JONES", np.nan, "WILLIAMS"])

# NaN propagates but doesn't error
print(mixed.str.upper())
print(mixed.str.len())
```

### Filling Missing Values

```{python}
# Fill missing before string operations
mixed.fillna("UNKNOWN").str.upper()
```

## Quick Reference

| Task | pandas `.str` | stringr |
|------|---------------|---------|
| Uppercase | `.str.upper()` | `str_to_upper()` |
| Lowercase | `.str.lower()` | `str_to_lower()` |
| Title case | `.str.title()` | `str_to_title()` |
| Strip whitespace | `.str.strip()` | `str_trim()` |
| Contains | `.str.contains(pat)` | `str_detect(x, pat)` |
| Replace | `.str.replace(old, new)` | `str_replace(x, old, new)` |
| Extract | `.str.extract(pat)` | `str_extract(x, pat)` |
| Split | `.str.split(sep)` | `str_split(x, sep)` |
| Length | `.str.len()` | `str_length(x)` |
| Starts with | `.str.startswith(s)` | `str_starts(x, s)` |
| Ends with | `.str.endswith(s)` | `str_ends(x, s)` |

## Exercise {#exercise}

::: {.exercise-box}
#### Exercise 8: Cleaning Division Names

1. Create a DataFrame with 5 division names in abbreviated format
2. Write a function that expands all abbreviations to full words
3. Create a normalized version for comparison (lowercase, consistent spacing)
4. Extract the first word of each division name
5. Check which division names contain "Science" or "Medicine"
6. Clean jobcodes by removing trailing letter suffixes (U, A, X, N)

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import re

# 1. Create data with abbreviated divisions
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "division": [
        "Coll of Ltrs & Sci",
        "Sch of Med & Pub Hlth",
        "Coll of Engr",
        "Sch of Bus",
        "Coll of Ag & Life Sci"
    ],
    "jobcode": ["FA001U", "AS002A", "FA003X", "CL001N", "ET002"]
})
print("1. Original data:")
print(df)

# 2. Abbreviation expansion function
def expand_abbreviations(text):
    expansions = {
        r"\bColl\b": "College",
        r"\bLtrs\b": "Letters",
        r"\bSci\b": "Science",
        r"\bSch\b": "School",
        r"\bMed\b": "Medicine",
        r"\bPub\b": "Public",
        r"\bHlth\b": "Health",
        r"\bEngr\b": "Engineering",
        r"\bBus\b": "Business",
        r"\bAg\b": "Agricultural"
    }
    for pattern, replacement in expansions.items():
        text = re.sub(pattern, replacement, text)
    return text

df["expanded"] = df["division"].apply(expand_abbreviations)
print("\n2. Expanded names:")
print(df[["division", "expanded"]])

# 3. Normalized version
def normalize(text):
    text = text.lower()
    text = text.replace("&", "and")
    text = " ".join(text.split())  # Normalize whitespace
    return text

df["normalized"] = df["expanded"].apply(normalize)
print("\n3. Normalized:")
print(df[["expanded", "normalized"]])

# 4. Extract first word
df["first_word"] = df["expanded"].str.split().str[0]
print("\n4. First word:")
print(df[["expanded", "first_word"]])

# 5. Contains Science or Medicine
df["has_science"] = df["expanded"].str.contains("Science")
df["has_medicine"] = df["expanded"].str.contains("Medicine")
print("\n5. Contains Science or Medicine:")
print(df[["expanded", "has_science", "has_medicine"]])

# 6. Clean jobcodes
df["jobcode_clean"] = df["jobcode"].str.replace(r"[UAXN]$", "", regex=True)
print("\n6. Cleaned jobcodes:")
print(df[["jobcode", "jobcode_clean"]])
```
:::
:::

## Summary

You've learned to manipulate strings in pandas:

- **`.str` accessor**: Vectorized string operations
- **Case conversion**: `upper()`, `lower()`, `title()`
- **Pattern matching**: `contains()`, `startswith()`, `endswith()`
- **Replacement**: `replace()` with strings or regex
- **Extraction**: `extract()` with regex groups
- **Splitting/joining**: `split()`, `join()`, concatenation

Key differences from R:
- pandas uses `.str.method()` syntax vs stringr's `str_method(x, ...)`
- Regex is built into pandas string methods
- Method chaining is natural in pandas

## Next Steps

Continue to [Lesson 9: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values](09-inflation-adjustment.qmd) to learn how to adjust salaries for inflation using CPI data.
